And  in everyday business  life  the Negro in Texas  in  1950
daily  rubs  shoulders  with  Anglo  and  Latin-Americans  in
Texas.  The  President's  executive  order  9980  governing  fair
employment:  practices  within  the  federal.  establishment  ef-
“fectively  prohibits  “discrimination  because of race, ‘color,
‘  religions  or  national  origin”  in  federal  employment.  in
Fexas  as  well  as  chroughout  the nation.**  Pursuant  thereto,
Negroes  work with whites in-the  federal  service.  In Dallas,
;  Houston,  and  other  Texas Cities,  Negro  mail  carriers and

clerks work  with  whites. 33  Negro. _— protect life “and

' CONCLUSION
-Fitst-class  citizenship  for Negroes is  an  impossibility
until  this Court recognizes  that the rule  of Plessy  v.  Fergu-
som  is ae anachronism  which  cannot stand  before the  Four-
teenth  Amendment.

5

:

~ aes mae

?

Despite the  facts that the  rule of that  case  has  necessarily
pS magnified  the  social inertia  and  the  prejudice  which is  the  ~
patrimony  of  many.  white Texans,  and  that  under  the rule’
_  of that  case  normal  and natural  associations  of free  people»
_have  been declared  unlawful, _ strides in  racial integra-
tion: are  now  evident.

<

+

The social  patterns in ‘Texas  and  the Secish today are  a
far  cry  from the  conditions  which  brought  forth  Plessy :
= Ferguson. ‘Now,  not only have social  scientists  scotched  the
myth of white  supremacy, but  Texans  in everyday  contact.
récognize  that  the  Negro is, entitled  toa  place of dignity in
-  a  democracy.  If  the  Constitution  does  not, ag. Mr.  Justice
‘Murphy  suggested in his  concurring.  opinion  in-Oyama  v.
California,?°  “render  irrational  as  a  justification  for  ae
crimination, those  factors  which  reflect.  racial  animosity,
the social-sclencise and  the daily experiences  of Texans  have
done so.

Pa

We  are  confident that: this Court will  consider  che "rea-
sonableness”  of  the exercise  of  Texas’  police  power  in  the -
|  — of  current  economic  and  sociological  conditions. $7

38 332 US 633 at 663.

37 Cf. wv est  Coast Hotel  v.  Parrish, 300  US  379.

22

{

_

_  There  appearing  AO.  reasonable  basis for enforced Segre-
gation on  the basis of race  at thie graduate school level; anc!
lz
to the contrary,  it appearing  that such  segregation  deprives  (
Negroes  of basic  rights guaranteed  by the  Constitution,  we  —
respectfully  submit  thaf  the  Texas  Court’s  denial  of  the
petitioner’s  right  iS full  and  unsegregated  educational  op-
portunity  should  be  reversed.  The  record  in  this case,  the
judgment of social  scientists,  and  the  daily  experience  of
Texans make  it  quite apparent.  that  Texans  are  Prepared.  |
for such  a  decision.

woe AFFIDAVIT

Herman. A. Barnett, a Negro — Lockhart,  Texas,  .
hi am n
I am.  now  a médical  student ‘at  the  University  of Texas
Medical  Branch at  Galveston,  Texas.  Even  though  I  am
the  only  Negro  student’  enrolled  in  this  medical.  school,
‘I  have  had  20  unpleasant.  experiences  during  any  of my
‘contacts  with either  the  other  students or  the faculty.

-

From. the very  first day I was as accepted as  just  another
-  student.  I met  most  of  the members  of  the freshman  class
day,
during  the. first  day  of orientation.  Also  on  the  first
‘several  of the  studénts  invited me  to  work.with  them  as)
a  laboratory. partner,  and one of the upper  classmen invited  »
-  me  out  to  his  home to  participate  with several  other fresh-
‘men. in-a/ study’ grat . which  he  was s  leading.
-  Because  of the way in which I bine been received  by. the
personnel  of  the  University: of  Texas. ‘Medical  School,  a
.  ~~ ath able “to devote  all my time  and energy to  the  study of.

|

medicine without  any  fear  of ill-will  or  rebuke.

|

APPENDIX D.
“Report  of Jak Negro  Student eae
a  Law School.  Duieersity of Oklahoma  —

STATE OF  OKLAHOMA  t  erie.

_-  COUNTY  OF GRADY  = coe  AFFIDAVIT

Wea name  is Ada  Lois  Sipuel Fisher.  I was  the plaintiff
in  the Sipuel v.  Oklahoma  University  case,  and  am  now  a  *
student:  in  the  School  of Law at the University  of Okla-  |
homa.

After  the Oklahoma  Legistacure  passed a  law allowing
Negroes  to  attend,  on  a segregated basis,  certain  Oklahoma  —

institutions  of higher learning, a about  50  other  Negro stu-
dents  and  I entered the  University  at  Norman in  June,  1949.
|  I was  the only one  to  enroll  in the law  school.

—

In spite. of the provisions  in  the ‘state  statutes  that  we
be provided  separate  classrooms  and instructors,  I attended  .
classes’ with  all  the other  entering  freshmen.  The semblance
of segregation  was  maintained  by “Reserved  for Colored”
signs  placed  beside  my  desk.  These  signs  did not  keep  my
classmates  from-coming  back  to  talk  with  me.and  exchang- |
ing class notes.  Gradually,  the  studerits  took  down the  si gns.  -
Froin  the  first,  my, classmates  were friendly.  Now  I  am  -

.

,  accepted  as  any other  member of  the class.

|

_  The ies source  of ‘embarrass ‘it,  both  to  me  and  my
classmates,  has .been  the  requirement  of  segregation.  ‘In
spite  of  this  tequireméent,  however,  we  study  together  in
the  ‘library,  and -on’  the  lawn  under  the.  trees,  have “buM
sessions”  in  the  halls and  on  the  steps  of  the  law  school,
and use  the  same  drinking  fountains  and  rest  rooms.  There

_  is no  segregation  whatever  in ou}  class  meetings  and  activi-
ties:  This  fall:che  summer  freshmen  endeavored  to  get  me
elected.  as  class  treasurer,  but  the  fall  freshmen  outnum-
_  bered  us  and — all the  class  offices.  I lost by about  10

votes.

!

The other Negro students and  I eat  in  the  Union “Jug”
at  a table reserved.  for  us  at the  back.  All during the  day,
other students  either  sit at our  table or pull their tables  near

-  ours  in order  that  we  can talk  while  having  coffee.

ee
Not  only am  I accepted  i in  the School of  Lew, but I have
been  treated  very  kindly  by white  families in  Norman  who
\  have  invited  other Negro students  and  me  to  their homes

for dinner.

.

I am  happy  finally-co  be enrolled  in the  State  Univer-
sity,  both  because  it  is  a\good  school  and  because  it  is
located  near  my  home,  consequently  less expensive  for  me
to  attend.

:

ADA Lois  SIPUEL  FISHER  —

Sworn  to and subscribed  before  me  this  17th.day  of  Feb-

ruary,  1950,  in  the city  of Chickasha, ee
Oklahoma.

he

APPENDIX. E

—_— of  First Negro Deities to Enter  the
Medical School,  University  of Arkansas

“<a

_  TO  WHOM  IT  MAY  CONCERN:

Being  the  first  Negro  student  admitted  to the. Schoo! of  |

Medicine  of  Arkansas  University,  the  other  students  seem
to.  have  gone  out  of  their  way  to  make  me  feel  that  I am
welcomed,  On  the  very  first  day,  September  27,  1948,  all  .
three  of the  white  girls and about  a third of the  white  boys
who also  were entering  as  freshmen,  made  it a  special  point
to  meet  me.  I  remember  the  seating  arrangement  at  our
very  first  class:  one  of  the white  boys  was  sitting  to  ‘my’
left;  and  one  of  the  girls  to my  right.  From  thei  on,  I
have had  no  fear  that the students  would  resent  my  pres-
ence.

‘

Here  at  the  Medical  School,  we-have  had  no  problem
-  with  the matter  of segregation,  like  my  friend,  Jackie Shrop-
shite,  had  when he entered  the Law School  at  the University
in  Fayetteville,  “Arkansas.  Here  there  was  no  attempt  to
“fence in”  my desk,  as  they  did  his  for  the  first few  days,
before  his  fellow  students tore  down the  railing.

Since  we  do  not  hneive any  study  halls in  the Medical
School,  we  usually  study in  each  other’s apartments.  The
three other  gitls,  all  residerts  ofArkansas,  have  become
.  very  close  friends  of  mine.  Several  of  the  fellows  and  I.
often  during the day go to  one  of the’ girl’s apartment  near
the  school  to discuss  and  study  our  lessons.  About  every
other  evening,  after.  classes,  two  of the  girls  and  I go  by  ~~’

the  grocery  store,  and  take  food  to  my  apartment  or  to  |

m-<

one  of  theirs, where we  prepare  our  ev, ening ssc before :
—e together late  into  the  ~—

te
.At  noon, /those of us  who  take  our  lunches  eat  together
one, of the rooms  at  the school.  Oftentimes,  after  classes,
I go with  several  of the  students  to  “drive-in”  cafes to  eat.

t \, ReMi:

hee.

I have  met  so  many fine  people,  here in  Little  Rock sual
at  the  Merical  School,  that I have  been having a  good  time.
Not a single  person  has  been  unkind to  me.

:

=
ae |

Rock,  a.  on  February  10,  1950.

'  Excerpt han the Journal of  Negro Education

Vol.  XIX, No.  1,  Page 4, Winter,  1950
_  EDITORIAL  COMMENT

SOME  PROGRESS  IN  THE  ELIMINATION OF.
DISCRIMINATION  IN HIGHER  EDUCATION

.  | INTHE UNITED STATES

Elimination  of Secregation  at. the  University Level

;

in the South —

Probably.the  most  encouraging  step  which. has been taken
in  this  area  has  been  the  admission  ‘of  Negroes  to several
_  Southern  state-supported  graduate  and  professional  schools
__..  which  hitherto  excluded  them  because  of  their  race.  And  —
~-what-is-more- important,  there- has not “been  reported a

single  untoward  incident  of  any  kind  as a  result  of  the
change.  Prior  to  the  school  year  1947-48,  only  two. state-
supported  universities  in the South had admitted  a  Negro
student  for  more  than  50- years.  The  law  school  of  the
University  of  Maryland  admitted  a  Negro  student  -in  1935,
-  and  around  ten  years ago  the  State  University  of  West
Virginia  began  to  admit  Negro  students’ to  its  graduate
and  professional  curricula.  In: each  case  the  experiment
proved  successful  beyond  anyone’s  expectations,  and  Ne-  _
groes have  since become. a normal  part  of the  student’ popu-
lation.

More recently, si state- suported universities  in fiyg, Southern
states  have  admitted  Negroes  to  various  schools  and-col-——
leges.  (1)  The University  of  Arkansas  in  1947-48  snag
a Negro to  its  law  school  on  a  segregated  basis.  The next °
4

eee

iets

Sh

33

;

:

:

oe

Ss ws

year  the  University  adntitted  a  Negro  girl  to its medical
.school  on  a’ non-segrtgated  basis,  and eliminated  the  seg-
regation  initially  imposed  in the  law  school.  (2)  The  Uni:
versity  of  Delaware  announced  in  1948  that  it had. tevised
its  policies so  as to permit  the admission  of Negro  students.
to any curricula  available  at  the University  which  were  not.
available  at  the  Negro  college  atDover.  A dozen  or  more
_ ‘Negroes  attended  the  University  last summer  and  several
»  are  at  present  enrolled.  (3)  The ‘University  of  Oklahoma, -
under  a  directive  of  the  Board of Regents,  admitted  a  Ne-*
gro  student on-a  segregated basis  to  its  graduate  school in
-  1948.  A little  later  Negro  students  were  enrolled in  some
of the professional  schools  on  a non- -segregated  basis.  (The
case of the  student  admitted  on  a  segregated  basis. is  now
before  the  U.  S.  Supreme  Court  to determine  the  consti-
tutional  validity  of  the  practice.)7  (4)  The  University  of
Kentucky  admitted  Negro students  to  its  graduate  school
last  year  for  the first  time,  enrolled a  sizeable  number in
its  summer  session, and  has several  enrolled  at: ‘the  present”
time.  (5)  The  University  of Texas  this  year  admitted  a_
Negro  to  its  medical  school  courses  at  Galveston,  but  te-
quired  him  to  register  as  a  student  i in  the Texas State  Uni-  -
|
versity  for Negroes  in Houston.

The  instances  cited hefe all  involve  state-supported  uni-  —

‘  versities  in the South  which hitherto had  excluded  Negroes.

In all,  state  universities in some séven Southern  and  border  —
states  now  admit  Negro  students.  to  vatious  schools  and.
collegés  within  their  university  organizations.  It  might. be.
noted in passing  that  some  of  the  privately-controlled  uni:
‘versities  and  colleges  in Missouri,  North  Carolina, Virginia

a

o

7 See: McLaurin  v.  Board  of a of  the  University of  Okla:  :

~~  homa,  et  al.

:

tig  i

and  the  District  of Columbia  also  admit  Negroes;  thus  |
_  increasing  the  number  df states  in  this  category  to  ten  and
the  District  of  Columbia.  In  addition  to  the  fact  that  the’
‘number  of such  instances is increasing,  the  important  point  .
to  be rioted  here is  not  why  these  institutions  have  changed
their  admission  policies  and  practices,  but  rather  that,  hav-
ing changed  them,  none  of the  dire  predictions  which.  were  —
made  before-hand  has materialized.  As  a  professor  at the
University of Kentucky  observed:®  “University  of Kentucky
tried  Plan  A  this  summer.  Worked  out O.K.  as  far  ag + oe
learned.  The  sky did not  fall,  neither.did  any  of the build-
ings  _ down,  nor r  did any  of the  students  get  contami-

-

-_

“Secale of i attitudes  of  professors  in  Southern  uni:~
_-versities  and  colleges,  attention  is  called  to  a  poll  which

~——has

just been  completed  by the  Southern Conference Edu-  _

cational  Fund  and  printed  in  this number  of  the  Journal.®
More than two-thirds  (68%)  of the  teachers  in  state and
a  privately  supported  white  higher. institutions  in the  South

favor  the immediate  abolition  of segregation  on the  gradu-  .
ate and  professional  level.  And,  I might  add,  that the  atti-  .
aint of  white  students  i in |  these i institutions  are even  more.
favorable.

:

‘APPENDIX G  eee  /

Article Printed  in  The Dallas Morning source
February 2, 1950

:

(

_NEGRO  ATTACK ON SEGREGATION
By  Allen Deckwore

‘

Last year,  in speaking  to  Negro  graduates  of  the new
state  university  at  Houston,  the  late ee  H.. Jester
gaid:s.  >.

ee  rd

fons

:

“Heaven  is not reached  in a  single  bound.”

Yet, as  far as  the  Texas  Negro  may be from  Heaven,  he
has  taken  some  long  bounds  in  the  last  six  years.  These
bounds  have  been  longer than: oe OF-t
——  white,  probably: realizes.

;

oa

.  The effort  of  the  Texas  Negro  to  beak down  a
tion  can -be  divided  into three phases—political  ee
educational: equality and  social  equality.

— support by. legal talent  from the  East,  especially
through  the National  Association  for. Advancement  of Col-
ored  sus: ate is pr the Pa  cane ,

One victory ee is won.  That i is  on  the political ae.

Until 1944,  the  Negro  in  the South was  classed  with
Republican  politics.  It was  Abraham  Lincoln,  the Republi-  .
can,  who.  gave  the  Negro  his  freedom.  By tradition, the —
Democratic  party  of  the  South  was  the  party  of the  white  —
_.  man.  And the  Democratic  primary elections  were  limited -
_  «fo  whites.

|

36

‘The  United  States  Supreme  Court,  controlled  by  Roose-
velt  appointees,  ruled  in  1944  that  Negroes must  be  ad-
"  mitted  to  Democratic  primaries.  ‘That  reversed  the  Negro
political  fealty  in  Dixie,  For  he -was  not  unappreciative
of what  the  Democratic  party  under  Roosevelt  was  doing.
‘Had  not  the  Democratic  party done  what  the Republicans
had  merely  talked  about?.  This  appreciation  was  evident _
- shortly  after  the  Supreme  Court  ruling.  Dallas  Negroes  —

raised  mogéy  for  the  Roosevelt-Truman

Texas campaign.

Today,  Negroes  not  only  vote  in Texas  Democratic
_  primaries;  they  alsq  participate  in  precinct,  county  and
state  conventions.  They  sit  with  whites.  Segregation  has
been  wiped  out  at  Democratic  conventions,  Conservative
delegates  from  Dallas  ‘ and  several  other counties  were  |
kicked  out  of the convention  at  Fort’ Worth in  September,  -
1948.  As  the conservatives  walked  out,  they  saw  dozens
of Negroes  filing in to take the  seats  they had just vacated.
The Negro had-come a long. wey It couldn’t have oe
. four  years. before.

‘

:

'  Two  yeats after  the  Nesro won  political  equality me.
turned  to  educational  equality."

2

_Heman Maxie Sweatt entered  as  the main  figure  on that
scene.  Sweatt  wanted  to  enroll  in-the  University -of  Texas  —
. ga school.  When  denied that  right, Sweatt  sued.

&

/

eee

: District Juse Roy C. Atcher  of Asselin held ie the  State
of Texas must  (1)  provide  separate  but  equal  educational
Negroes, or (2) let them enter the univer-

‘sity for whities.”

Ss

| °

The oe the warning  and proceeded  to establggh
aniversity  for Negroes  at Houston.  The univer-

a  separate

e  -:

_sity is now  a  going  concern,  with  more  than  2,000  Negroes  -

ve  es there.

“In  suite to meet  requiremenss  for Sweatt,  the university
set up a temporary and ‘Separate law school near the Capitol -
grounds  in 1  Austin.

P

;

ae
ean

:

y,

*  Sweatt did  t not eel  at  either  the  Houston  university

nor  at  the sees law ‘school at Austin.

:

|

His  reason  became  obvious. in  later trials and appeals

of  his lawsuit.

Atty. Gea. Price Daniel eee to  show  in  the court-
room  that’ it was  not  the purpose  of Sweatt  and  his  attor-
neys  (from the  National  Association  for  Advancement  of
Colored People) to get ‘Sweatt an  education  alone.  They
“wanted  Sweatt  in a white  university,  Daniel  argued.  The  -
Daniel  argument  was  upheld: in Sweatt’s  own  testimony.

&,-

Sweatt  said  he would  not  enter  a separate ‘school  for
Negroes,  even  though  it ‘might. be  adjudged  = to  the
:

-  inain University  of Texas law  ani

,

“I  don’t  believe in  segregation,”  Sweatt  said.

—— a  ———

The Negro litigation on ‘ddiicinign

third phase  of the  NAACP  campaign—the drive  foz  social’ -
equality,  as  witness Sweatt’s  own  statement  on  segregation.

oe  een

tied  closely to  the

Latest  on  the antisegregation suits  involves’ not  politics,
Ror education,  but  recreation.  Negroes  now  are.  demanding
equa!”  facilities  at- state  parks. The  legal  argument of  the
Negroes is  ee by the  State | — Board. Riad board  |

eds

38

has. gedeced  state  parks | hie this  year unless means  can
be found to Larumumnaued Negroes.  °

|

The NAACP  lawyers follow  7 a smart ‘path. They  believe
they can  make  segregation  too  expensive. By  potshotting

University. of ‘Texas  might  have  to  admit  that’ Negro at_
the  El  Paso branch  or  build  an  entirely  separate  plant  for
him.  In  order  to  ‘set  up  a:  segregated  mining  schodl,  the  |
Negro lawyers estimate, the university would  have tu  spend
$5,000,000,  And that’s  a  lot  of dollars nor one. a.

Report  on  Interracial  Activities

Ste,

The. Texas Methodist  Student  Movement,  with  contacts
_in  over  fifty  local campus  groups,  has  beert  interested  in
interracial  work  for several  years.  This  is partly  due  to  a

~  real‘concern  on  the-part-of  white  students  and  leaders  for

the religious  welfare  of students  in  Negro’ colleges.  It  is ;

also a response  to the  action  of the  last General  Conference
-  of  The  Methodist  Church,  calling ‘on  Methodists  to work
toward the elimination  of segregation  in their  church meet-

This work  has  been  most  evident  i in the anhual  Thanks-
: giving  Conferences  held  by  the  movement,  beginning  in
'.  Denton  in  1944,  when,  one.  of  the  leaders. was  a  Negro  -
minister  and  educator.  In  1945  the  conference  was  held
in  Corsicana,  again with  one Negro speaker and  this time
with  four or  five  Negro  students. attending  sessions and
eating. with  the white  students.  Since  this  time,  provisions  .
have  been  made  for  each.  conference  to  be  held  interra-
cially;  however,  no Negro  delegates attended  tle 1946  con-
ference at  Hillsboro.  The  1947  conference  was  held in  Aus-_
_  tin,  with  over  twenty  Negro  delegates  from  two.  colleges  —
in  attendance.  Some  students  were  housed  jnterracially,  all
delegates  ateftogether  in  a  public “white”  cafeteria,  and
_  sessions  were  held in  the campus church.  In  1948  one  Negro
